title: Chromogranin A (CgA)
subtitle: Neuroendocrine Tumor Biomarker — Diagnostic Performance, Confounders & Surveillance Limits, With a Skepticism Section
authors: Claude-researched synthesis, sourced directly from PubMed-indexed meta-analyses, prospective trials, and assay-comparison studies
sourceFile: Claude-researched (WebSearch), not user-pasted; primary sources cited per item (PLOS One meta-analysis, CASPAR study/Clin Cancer Res 2024, PPI-confounding studies, assay-comparison studies, NETest head-to-head studies)

items.title: Why is chromogranin A useful as a biomarker for neuroendocrine tumors despite being a fairly nonspecific protein?
items.content:
Chromogranin A is co-secreted with peptide hormones from neuroendocrine secretory granules across many neuroendocrine cell types, so its blood concentration broadly tracks neuroendocrine secretory activity rather than any single hormone.
This breadth is what makes it useful as a general screening marker across diverse NET types (gastroenteropancreatic, bronchopulmonary, pheochromocytoma), but the same breadth is also why so many non-neoplastic conditions that increase neuroendocrine cell activity (or impair its clearance) can also elevate it.
keyPoints: CgA is co-secreted from neuroendocrine secretory granules broadly, not tumor-specifically.
This makes it a useful general NET screening marker across multiple tumor types.
The same nonspecificity is the root of most of its false-positive problems.
items.reference: General chromogranin A biology underlying its use as a pan-neuroendocrine marker (PMC3712684, single-center experience and literature review).

items.title: What is chromogranin A's pooled diagnostic accuracy for neuroendocrine tumor diagnosis?
items.content: A meta-analysis pooling 13 studies (1,260 NET patients vs 967 controls) found an overall sensitivity of 73% and specificity of 95% for NET diagnosis, with a diagnostic odds ratio of 56.29 and an AUC of 0.896 — indicating good but not excellent discrimination, driven more by its high specificity than by its sensitivity.
keyPoints: Pooled sensitivity 73%, specificity 95% (13 studies, 2,227 subjects).
AUC 0.896 — good discrimination overall.
High specificity, only moderate sensitivity.
items.reference: "Diagnostic Value of Circulating Chromogranin A for Neuroendocrine Tumors: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis," PLOS One (journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0124884).

items.title: How does chromogranin A's diagnostic sensitivity vary across different neuroendocrine tumor subtypes?
items.content:
CgA's sensitivity is not uniform across NET subtypes: it performs better for midgut NETs (~68% sensitivity) than for pancreatic NETs (~54%), and worse still for small-cell lung cancer (~59.9% sensitivity, 79.4% specificity).
For non-insulinoma pancreatic NETs specifically, a cutoff of 74 ng/mL yields 65.6% sensitivity and 91.9% specificity — but CgA performs poorly for insulinomas, which often secrete relatively little CgA.
keyPoints: Midgut NET sensitivity ~68% vs pancreatic NET ~54%.
SCLC: sensitivity 59.9%, specificity 79.4%.
Non-insulinoma PNET (cutoff 74 ng/mL): sensitivity 65.6%, specificity 91.9%; CgA performs poorly for insulinomas specifically.
items.reference: Bronchopulmonary NEN meta-analysis (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31266019); "Chromogranin A is a reliable serum diagnostic biomarker for pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors but not for insulinomas" (PMC4130880).

items.title: What is chromogranin A's diagnostic performance for pheochromocytoma and paraganglioma?
items.content: Across studies, CgA's sensitivity for pheochromocytoma ranges roughly 83-90% with specificity 96-99% when compared against plasma catecholamines/metanephrines, though estimates vary meaningfully by study and population (see Skepticism section). Combining CgA with plasma catecholamine testing improves overall specificity to about 95% with excellent positive predictive value.
keyPoints: Pheochromocytoma sensitivity ~83-90%, specificity ~96-99% (varies by study).
Combination with plasma catecholamines improves specificity to ~95%.
Newer metanephrine assays generally outperform CgA alone for this indication.
items.reference: Original sensitivity/specificity description (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/1988765); pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma diagnostic review (mdpi.com/2072-6694/11/4/586).

items.title: Why must proton pump inhibitors be discontinued before drawing a chromogranin A level?
items.content:
PPIs cause chronic hypergastrinemia, which provokes hyperplasia of enterochromaffin-like (ECL) cells in the stomach. These hyperplastic ECL cells secrete CgA, producing an elevation that has nothing to do with a neuroendocrine tumor.
This is the single most common cause of a falsely elevated CgA encountered in practice.
keyPoints: PPIs → chronic hypergastrinemia → ECL cell hyperplasia → CgA secretion.
This is unrelated to any actual neuroendocrine neoplasm.
PPI use is the most common cause of false-positive CgA elevation.
items.reference: "False elevation of chromogranin A due to proton pump inhibitors" (ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21527812).

items.title: How much does stopping a PPI typically reduce a falsely elevated chromogranin A, and how long should it be stopped?
items.content: In a prospective study of 196 patients with known well-differentiated NETs, about 10% had elevated CgA attributable to PPI use, and stopping the PPI produced an average 82% decrease in CgA level; other studies report 81-89% reductions. Current practice is to discontinue PPIs for at least 2 weeks before drawing a CgA sample whenever feasible.
keyPoints: PPI discontinuation: ~82-89% reduction in falsely elevated CgA in known NET patients.
Recommended PPI washout before testing: at least 2 weeks.
A multigene PCR test (NETest) remained negative in over 80% of these same PPI-treated patients while CgA stayed elevated.
items.reference: PPI discontinuation study in NET patients (ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3208502); multigene-test PPI confounder comparison (ec.bioscientifica.com/view/journals/ec/3/4/215.xml).

items.title: Why does renal impairment confound chromogranin A interpretation?
items.content:
The kidney is CgA's main clearance route. As glomerular filtration rate falls, CgA accumulates in plasma independent of any tumor — meaning a patient with CKD can have a markedly elevated CgA purely from reduced clearance, not from neuroendocrine disease.
This is also why CgA's specificity for pheochromocytoma specifically drops in patients with renal dysfunction, and why GFR-adjusted reference ranges have been proposed for accurate interpretation.
keyPoints: Kidney is the primary clearance route for CgA.
Reduced GFR → CgA accumulation independent of tumor presence.
Pheochromocytoma-diagnostic specificity of CgA drops in renal dysfunction.
items.reference: "Diagnostic specificity of serum chromogranin-A for pheochromocytoma in patients with renal dysfunction" (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8175970); renal disease pathway review (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31714338).

items.title: What other common conditions can cause a false-positive chromogranin A elevation, beyond PPIs and renal impairment?
items.content: Chronic atrophic gastritis (via the same gastrin/ECL-hyperplasia mechanism as PPIs), inflammatory bowel disease, hepatic impairment, and several cardiovascular conditions (heart failure, hypertension, acute coronary syndrome) are all recognized causes of CgA elevation unrelated to any neuroendocrine neoplasm.
keyPoints: Chronic atrophic gastritis: same ECL-hyperplasia mechanism as PPIs.
Inflammatory bowel disease and hepatic impairment also elevate CgA.
Heart failure, hypertension, and acute coronary syndrome are recognized cardiovascular confounders.
items.reference: "Rethinking chromogranin A: unveiling gastrointestinal factors beyond neuroendocrine neoplasms" (pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12314672).

items.title: Why can't chromogranin A values be reliably compared across different commercial assay platforms?
items.content:
Commercial CgA assays use different antibodies with different epitope specificities and lack a shared, standardized reference material. In one head-to-head comparison, 35% of patients flagged as elevated by one assay (ELISA) were not flagged by another (IRMA), and vice versa. A separate comparison of three commercial kits found sensitivity/specificity ranging from 67%/96% to 93%/88% depending on the kit used.
This means a patient's CgA trend is only meaningful if every sample was run on the same assay platform.
keyPoints: No standardized reference material or antibody epitope across commercial CgA assays.
ELISA vs IRMA discordance affected ~35% of patients in one comparison.
Three-kit comparison: sensitivity/specificity ranged from 67%/96% to 93%/88%.
Serial monitoring requires using the same assay every time.
items.reference: Two-assay (ELISA vs IRMA) comparison (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15646836); three-commercial-kit comparison (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12740022).

items.title: How did the CASPAR study evaluate chromogranin A's role in NET surveillance, and what did it find?
items.content:
CASPAR was a prospective, multicenter, blinded study (153 patients, 612 visits, sites in the US and Germany) validating an automated CgA assay for detecting disease progression in GEP-NET patients, using a pre-specified threshold (≥50% rise to a value >100 ng/mL) compared against RECIST 1.1 imaging.
It found specificity 93.4%, sensitivity only 34.4%, PPV 57.9%, NPV 84.3%, and AUC 0.73 — meaning serial CgA is a reasonably good rule-out adjunct (high specificity/NPV) but a poor stand-alone tool for actually detecting progression (low sensitivity).
keyPoints: CASPAR: 153 patients, 612 visits, prospective multicenter design.
Progression-detection performance: sensitivity 34.4%, specificity 93.4%, NPV 84.3%, AUC 0.73.
Useful as a high-specificity adjunct to imaging, not as a standalone surveillance tool.
items.reference: "Circulating Chromogranin A as a Surveillance Biomarker in Patients with Carcinoids—The CASPAR Study," Clinical Cancer Research, 2024 (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39453770).

items.title: Why is chromogranin A a poor standalone tool for detecting NET recurrence despite its good specificity?
items.content:
A test's specificity tells you how well it avoids false alarms, but its sensitivity tells you how well it catches real disease — and CASPAR's 34.4% sensitivity for progression means roughly two-thirds of true progression events would be missed if CgA alone were relied upon for surveillance.
This is why CgA is positioned as an adjunct that can reasonably reassure (high NPV) when normal, but cannot substitute for periodic imaging.
keyPoints: 34.4% sensitivity means ~65% of true progression events would be missed by CgA alone.
High specificity/NPV makes it good for reassurance, not detection.
Imaging remains necessary; CgA cannot replace it for surveillance.
items.reference: CASPAR study, Clinical Cancer Research, 2024.

items.title: How does the NETest multianalyte blood test compare with chromogranin A head-to-head?
items.content: NETest, a 51-gene multianalyte PCR blood test, has been reported in head-to-head studies to outperform CgA substantially: one comparison found NETest >92% accurate versus CgA's ~76%; another found NETest sensitivity 98.4%/specificity 100%/AUC 0.997 versus CgA's AUC of only 0.54. NETest was also unaffected by PPI use, while CgA was elevated in roughly 80-83% of PPI-treated subjects in the same comparisons.
keyPoints: NETest accuracy >92% vs CgA ~76% in one comparison.
NETest AUC 0.997 vs CgA AUC 0.54 in another comparison.
NETest unaffected by PPIs; CgA elevated in 80-83% of PPI-treated subjects in the same studies.
items.reference: "A multianalyte PCR blood test outperforms single analyte ELISAs" (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25015994); "A PCR blood test outperforms chromogranin A in carcinoid detection" (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25316294).

items.title: Who economically benefits from chromogranin A and NETest testing?
items.content:
Reference labs (Quest, LabCorp, ARUP) bill roughly $160-240 per CgA test and benefit from repeat testing driven by the assay's frequent need for confounder workup and re-draws. IVD manufacturers (DAKO, CIS Bio, EuroDiagnostica) compete on unstandardized assays rather than a single dominant product.
Wren Laboratories markets NETest as a premium, single-source replacement for CgA, explicitly leading with CgA's PPI-confounding flaws in its own published comparisons — a commercial position with no cross-lab standardization problem, because there is only one lab running the test.
keyPoints: Reference labs bill ~$160-240/CgA test (cash price; varies by payer).
IVD manufacturers compete on unstandardized assays.
Wren Laboratories markets NETest as a premium single-source CgA replacement.
items.reference: CgA test cost listings (findlabtest.com); NETest commercialization (wrenlaboratories.com).

items.title: Why might an experienced clinician check PPI, renal, and gastritis status before acting on an elevated chromogranin A, when academic teaching often glosses over this?
items.content: Textbooks frequently present CgA as a clean tumor marker without foregrounding how dominant its confounders are in practice. A clinician who has been burned by a false alarm learns to treat a single elevated CgA as provisional until PPI use, renal function, and a history of atrophic gastritis or IBD are checked — since PPI discontinuation alone reverses 82-89% of "elevated" results in known NET patients.
keyPoints: Academic teaching often understates how common CgA confounders are in practice.
Practical workflow: check PPI use, renal function, gastritis/IBD history before acting.
PPI discontinuation alone reverses the majority of false elevations in known NET patients.
items.reference: PPI discontinuation reversal rates (ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3208502).

items.title: What is the best-supported current clinical use of chromogranin A, given its limitations?
items.content: Given pooled sensitivity/specificity of 73%/95% for diagnosis, poor early-stage pancreatic NET accuracy, and only 34.4% sensitivity for detecting progression on serial surveillance (per CASPAR), CgA is best used as a diagnostic adjunct alongside imaging and clinical suspicion — not as a standalone screening or surveillance tool — with PPI/renal/gastritis confounders actively excluded before interpreting any result.
keyPoints: Best use: diagnostic adjunct alongside imaging/clinical suspicion, not standalone.
Confounders must be actively excluded before interpreting a result.
Not a substitute for periodic imaging in surveillance.
items.reference: Synthesis of meta-analysis (PLOS One) and CASPAR study (Clin Cancer Res 2024) findings.

items.title: Why should NETest's reported superiority over chromogranin A be interpreted cautiously?
items.content:
The head-to-head studies showing NETest dramatically outperforming CgA (accuracy >92% vs ~76%; AUC 0.997 vs 0.54) are compelling, but most published NETest validation studies to date involve authors affiliated with Wren Laboratories, the company that commercializes the test.
This is not evidence the results are wrong, but a company-affiliated evidence base is a recognized source of bias in diagnostic-accuracy research, and independent, non-affiliated replication of these specific head-to-head numbers is more limited than the company's own publication volume might suggest.
keyPoints: Most NETest validation studies involve Wren Laboratories-affiliated authors.
Company-affiliated evidence is a recognized bias risk in diagnostic studies, not proof of wrongdoing.
Independent, non-affiliated replication of the specific head-to-head numbers is more limited.
items.reference: NETest head-to-head studies (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25015994; pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25316294) — author affiliations include Wren Laboratories/Yale collaborators commercializing the test.

items.title: Why is it inaccurate to say guidelines clearly recommend against routine chromogranin A use?
items.content:
It would be easy to read CgA's poor surveillance sensitivity (34.4% in CASPAR) as proof that guidelines must recommend against its routine use — but that overstates the actual guideline landscape. The CASPAR investigators themselves describe "current recommendations for using serum chromogranin A for patients with GEP-NET" as "equivocal," and a survey of ENETS centers of excellence found CgA screening practice varies by center rather than following one settled recommendation.
The accurate statement is that CgA's role is contested and inconsistently applied, not that it has been formally recommended against.
keyPoints: CASPAR authors describe current CgA recommendations for GEP-NET as "equivocal," not "against."
ENETS centers of excellence vary in their CgA screening practice.
"Contested/inconsistent" is accurate; "formally recommended against" is not.
items.reference: CASPAR study discussion (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39453770); ENETS center-of-excellence practice survey referenced in CgA-and-MEN1 screening literature (pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12370824).

items.title: Why do reported pheochromocytoma sensitivity/specificity figures for chromogranin A vary so widely between studies?
items.content:
Different studies report meaningfully different numbers for CgA in pheochromocytoma — e.g., one older study reports sensitivity 86%/specificity 74%, while a newer study reports sensitivity 90.2%/specificity 99.0%. These differences likely reflect differences in patient selection (familial vs sporadic disease, tumor size), the comparator used, and the assay platform — not a single fixed "true" sensitivity/specificity for the test.
Any single number quoted for CgA's pheochromocytoma performance should be treated as study-specific, not as a universal constant.
keyPoints: Reported sensitivity for pheochromocytoma ranges from ~83% to ~90% across studies; specificity from ~74% to ~99%.
Differences likely reflect patient selection, comparator test, and assay platform.
Treat any single quoted number as study-specific, not universal.
items.reference: Comparison of pheochromocytoma diagnostic studies (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/1988765; mdpi.com/2072-6694/11/4/586).

items.title: Why is chromogranin A's proposed role in actively causing CKD progression (not just reflecting it) still mechanistic rather than clinically proven?
items.content:
Some literature describes elevated CgA as not merely a marker of reduced renal clearance but as a potential pathogenic factor that promotes mesangial cell inflammation and fibrosis, contributing to CKD progression.
This causal claim is currently supported mainly by mechanistic/laboratory-level findings rather than large prospective clinical outcome trials demonstrating that lowering CgA itself slows CKD progression in humans — it should be treated as a plausible pathway under investigation, not an established clinical fact.
keyPoints: CgA-as-pathogenic-driver-of-CKD is a mechanistic, not yet a proven clinical-outcome, claim.
Evidence so far is largely laboratory/mechanistic (mesangial cell signaling).
No large prospective human outcome trial yet establishes CgA-lowering as CKD-protective.
items.reference: "Chromogranin A pathway: from pathogenic molecule to renal disease" (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31714338) — a mechanistic review, not an outcomes trial.

items.title: Why shouldn't a quoted $160-240 figure be treated as the representative real-world cost of chromogranin A testing?
items.content:
Cash/list prices of roughly $160-240 found on direct-to-consumer lab-test websites reflect self-pay retail pricing at a handful of vendors, not the actual insurance-negotiated reimbursement rate that most patients and payers experience, which can be substantially lower or higher depending on contract, region, and whether the test is bundled with other orders.
These figures are useful as a rough order-of-magnitude reference, not as a precise statement of "the cost" of CgA testing.
keyPoints: Quoted prices are direct-to-consumer cash/list prices, not typical insurance-negotiated rates.
Actual cost to a given patient/payer varies substantially by contract and region.
Treat the figure as order-of-magnitude only.
items.reference: Direct-to-consumer CgA test pricing listings (findlabtest.com) — not a claims/reimbursement data source.

items.question: A patient on long-term omeprazole for GERD has an incidentally elevated chromogranin A of 220 ng/mL with no symptoms suggestive of a neuroendocrine tumor. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Immediate CT imaging to search for an occult NET
Discontinue the PPI for at least 2 weeks and repeat the CgA level before pursuing further workup
Start empiric somatostatin analog therapy
Conclude the result is definitively normal and take no action
items.explanation: PPI use is the most common cause of false-positive CgA elevation via gastrin-driven ECL cell hyperplasia. Standard practice is to discontinue the PPI for at least 2 weeks and repeat the level before pursuing further workup, since this commonly reverses 82-89% of such elevations.
items.reference: PPI discontinuation study (ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3208502).

items.question: A patient with stage 4 chronic kidney disease (eGFR 22) has an elevated chromogranin A. What is the most likely explanation, independent of any neuroendocrine tumor?
options: CgA is not affected by renal function
The kidney is CgA's main clearance route, so reduced GFR alone can elevate plasma CgA
CKD always indicates an underlying occult NET
This combination is so rare it requires no special interpretation
items.explanation: The kidney is the primary clearance route for chromogranin A; reduced glomerular filtration rate alone causes CgA to accumulate in plasma independent of any neuroendocrine tumor, requiring careful interpretation or GFR-adjusted reference ranges.
items.reference: Renal disease pathway review (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31714338); pheochromocytoma specificity in renal dysfunction (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8175970).

items.question: A patient's chromogranin A was elevated at one lab using an ELISA assay but normal when repeated at a different lab using an IRMA assay. What is the most likely explanation?
options: A new tumor developed between the two tests
Commercial CgA assays use different antibodies without a shared standardized reference, causing real discordance between platforms (~35% in one comparison)
Laboratory error must have occurred; this discordance is never expected
IRMA assays are always more accurate than ELISA
items.explanation: Different commercial CgA assays use different antibody epitopes without a standardized reference material, producing genuine discordance between platforms — about 35% in one ELISA-vs-IRMA comparison. Serial monitoring requires using the same assay each time.
items.reference: ELISA vs IRMA comparison (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15646836).

items.question: Based on the CASPAR study, which statement best describes serial chromogranin A's role in detecting GEP-NET disease progression?
options: It is highly sensitive and can replace imaging entirely
It has high specificity/NPV (good for reassurance when normal) but low sensitivity (34.4%), so it cannot reliably detect true progression alone
It has no clinical value whatsoever for surveillance
It performs identically to imaging in detecting progression
items.explanation: CASPAR found serial CgA had specificity 93.4% and NPV 84.3% (useful for reassurance) but sensitivity of only 34.4% — meaning roughly two-thirds of true progression events would be missed if relied upon alone, so it cannot replace imaging.
items.reference: CASPAR study, Clinical Cancer Research, 2024.

items.question: A patient has a suspected insulinoma. How reliable is chromogranin A likely to be for this specific diagnosis?
options: Highly reliable — CgA performs best for insulinomas of all PNET subtypes
Unreliable — CgA is a poor marker specifically for insulinomas, even though it performs reasonably for other pancreatic NETs
CgA cannot be measured in patients with suspected insulinoma
CgA is 100% sensitive and specific for all pancreatic NET subtypes
items.explanation: Chromogranin A is a reliable marker for pancreatic NETs generally, but performs poorly specifically for insulinomas, which often secrete relatively little CgA.
items.reference: "Chromogranin A is a reliable serum diagnostic biomarker for pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors but not for insulinomas" (PMC4130880).

items.question: How does Wren Laboratories position its NETest product relative to chromogranin A in the market?
options: As a cheaper generic alternative to CgA
As a premium, single-source replacement, explicitly marketing CgA's PPI-confounding weakness as part of its sales case
As a test that must always be ordered alongside CgA, never instead of it
As a publicly available, non-proprietary test
items.explanation: Wren Laboratories markets NETest as a premium, single-source replacement for CgA, with its own published comparisons highlighting CgA's vulnerability to PPI-related false elevation as a key selling point.
items.reference: NETest commercialization (wrenlaboratories.com); head-to-head PPI-confounder comparison (ec.bioscientifica.com/view/journals/ec/3/4/215.xml).

items.question: A trainee claims that 'guidelines recommend against routine chromogranin A testing in GEP-NET surveillance.' What is the most accurate correction based on the CASPAR study's own discussion?
options: This statement is fully accurate as written
The CASPAR investigators describe current recommendations as "equivocal," not as a formal recommendation against use — practice varies by center rather than following one settled rule
Guidelines strongly recommend CgA as the primary surveillance tool, no imaging needed
No guideline body has ever commented on chromogranin A's surveillance role
items.explanation: The CASPAR study's own discussion describes current recommendations for serum CgA in GEP-NET patients as equivocal, and notes that CgA screening practice varies across ENETS centers of excellence — the evidence supports "contested/inconsistent," not a formal recommendation against use.
items.reference: CASPAR study (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39453770); ENETS center practice variation (pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12370824).

items.question: Head-to-head studies report NETest dramatically outperforming chromogranin A (e.g., AUC 0.997 vs 0.54). What important caveat should temper how confidently this is cited?
options: No caveat is needed; the result is definitively settled
Most of these validation studies involve authors affiliated with Wren Laboratories, the company commercializing NETest — a recognized bias risk pending broader independent replication
The studies were retracted and should not be cited at all
NETest has never been compared directly to CgA in any published study
items.explanation: Most published NETest head-to-head validation studies involve authors affiliated with Wren Laboratories, the company that commercializes the test — a recognized source of bias in diagnostic-accuracy research that warrants caution pending more independent, non-affiliated replication, even though it doesn't necessarily mean the findings are wrong.
items.reference: NETest head-to-head studies (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25015994; pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25316294).

items.question: Which factor most improves chromogranin A's diagnostic specificity for pheochromocytoma in clinical practice?
options: Measuring CgA alone at a higher cutoff
Combining CgA with plasma catecholamine/metanephrine testing
Repeating the CgA test three times on the same day
Measuring CgA only in patients already on PERT
items.explanation: Combining CgA testing with plasma catecholamine testing improves overall specificity to about 95% with excellent positive predictive value, better than either test used alone.
items.reference: Pheochromocytoma combination-testing data (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/1988765).

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a recognized cause of a false-positive chromogranin A elevation?
options: Chronic atrophic gastritis
Inflammatory bowel disease
Acute coronary syndrome
A single cup of coffee on the morning of the blood draw
items.explanation: Chronic atrophic gastritis, inflammatory bowel disease, and acute coronary syndrome are all recognized causes of CgA elevation unrelated to neuroendocrine neoplasia. Routine caffeine intake is not a recognized confounder.
items.reference: "Rethinking chromogranin A: unveiling gastrointestinal factors beyond neuroendocrine neoplasms" (pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12314672).
